commit 2e258b44b6be21fecdf75c0eb5bfc7194ba0b0e7
Author: Deepanshu Kartikey <kartikey406@gmail.com>

kernel/fork: validate exit_signal in kernel_clone()

This commit moves the validation of exit_signal from
copy_clone_args_from_user() into kernel_clone() to ensure all callers,
including legacy clone() syscalls, are protected. It now rejects invalid
exit_signal values (values greater than _NSIG) with -EINVAL.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260307064202.353405-1-kartikey406@gmail.com/T/ [v1]
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260316104536.558108-1-kartikey406@gmail.com/T/ [v2]

> diff --git a/kernel/fork.c b/kernel/fork.c
> index 65113a304518..59a790d1b257 100644
> --- a/kernel/fork.c
> +++ b/kernel/fork.c
> @@ -2633,6 +2633,9 @@ pid_t kernel_clone(struct kernel_clone_args *args)
>  	    (args->pidfd == args->parent_tid))
>  		return -EINVAL;
>  
> +	if (!valid_signal(args->exit_signal))
> +		return -EINVAL;
> +

Is the documentation comment directly above kernel_clone() still accurate?

It currently states that args->exit_signal is expected to be checked for
sanity by the caller, but this check shifts the validation responsibility
away from the caller.

Also, does this change introduce a user-visible regression for legacy clone()
callers?

Prior to this patch, the legacy clone() syscall silently accepted invalid
signals up to 255 in the lower byte of clone_flags, treating them as if no
signal should be sent.

By moving the check into kernel_clone(), these previously accepted calls
will now fail with -EINVAL. Could this break existing userspace applications
that inadvertently set bits in the 65-255 range due to uninitialized memory
or buggy bitwise operations?

Target Commit:
commit 2e258b44b6be21fecdf75c0eb5bfc7194ba0b0e7
Author: Deepanshu Kartikey <kartikey406@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Mar 16 15:19:56 2026 +0000

    kernel/fork: validate exit_signal in kernel_clone()
    
    When a child process exits, it sends exit_signal to its parent via
    do_notify_parent(). The clone() syscall constructs exit_signal as:
    
    (lower_32_bits(clone_flags) & CSIGNAL)
    
    CSIGNAL is 0xff, so values in the range 65-255 are possible. However,
    valid_signal() only accepts signals up to _NSIG (64 on x86_64). A
    non-zero non-valid exit_signal acts the same as exit_signal == 0:
    the parent process is not signaled when the child terminates.
    
    The syzkaller reproducer triggers this by calling clone() with
    flags=0x80, resulting in exit_signal = (0x80 & CSIGNAL) = 128, which
    exceeds _NSIG and is not a valid signal.
    
    The v1 of this patch added the check only in the clone() syscall
    handler, which is incomplete. kernel_clone() has other callers such
    as sys_ia32_clone() which would remain unprotected. Move the check
    to kernel_clone() to cover all callers.
    
    Since the valid_signal() check is now in kernel_clone() and covers
    all callers including clone3(), the same check in
    copy_clone_args_from_user() becomes redundant and is removed. The
    higher 32bits check for clone3() is kept as it is clone3() specific.
    
    Note that this is a user-visible change: previously, passing an invalid
    exit_signal to clone() was silently accepted. The man page for clone()
    does not document any defined behavior for invalid exit_signal values,
    so rejecting them with -EINVAL is the correct behavior. It is unlikely
    that any sane application relies on passing an invalid exit_signal.
    
    Reported-by: syzbot+bbe6b99feefc3a0842de@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
    Closes: https://syzkaller.appspot.com/bug?extid=bbe6b99feefc3a0842de
    Tested-by: syzbot+bbe6b99feefc3a0842de@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
    Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260307064202.353405-1-kartikey406@gmail.com/T/ [v1]
    Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260316104536.558108-1-kartikey406@gmail.com/T/ [v2]
    Fixes: 3f2c788a1314 ("fork: prevent accidental access to clone3 features")
    Signed-off-by: Deepanshu Kartikey <Kartikey406@gmail.com>

Okay, let's break this down. I see this `sigaddset(&pending->signal, sig);` call, and immediately my expert eyes focus on the value of `sig`. It's 128. That's immediately a red flag. The `sigaddset` function, as I recall, does a bitwise operation on a signal set, and it's indexing using `sig - 1`.

Looking at the code:

```c
static inline void sigaddset(sigset_t *set, int _sig)
{
	unsigned long sig = _sig - 1;
	if (_NSIG_WORDS == 1)
		set->sig[0] |= 1UL << sig;
	else
		set->sig[sig / _NSIG_BPW] |= 1UL << (sig % _NSIG_BPW);
}
```

The problem is staring me right in the face. With `sig` being 128, `sig - 1` becomes 127. If we're on a 64-bit system, where `_NSIG_WORDS` is 1 (implying a 64-bit signal set), that means `1UL << 127`. That's *undefined behavior*! I know that. It's shifting by more than the bit width of `unsigned long`. And even if the compiler *masks* the shift amount, as it might on x86_64, it's still corrupting the signal set: in this case, the `1UL << 127` would actually become `1UL << 63`, effectively setting the 64th signal in the set.  That's a bit dangerous.

And if we're on a 32-bit system (`_NSIG_WORDS` equals 2), it looks even worse. The code becomes `set->sig[3] |= 1UL << 31`. Hold on... `set->sig` only has *two* elements! So, that's an *out-of-bounds write*! A classic buffer overflow, pure and simple, leading to memory corruption!

So, the crux of the issue is that if `exit_signal` can be greater than 64, this whole process is vulnerable to `sigaddset` corrupting memory when a child exits and sends a signal to its parent. That's a *critical* bug! It's a memory corruption vulnerability. The patch they're talking about, it seems, is fixing this by checking `!valid_signal(args->exit_signal)`, limiting it to values less than or equal to 64.  Absolutely crucial fix.

I was focusing on how `clone3()` used to handle `exit_signal`. The code in `copy_clone_args_from_user` seems to have *already* been rejecting invalid exit signals with `-EINVAL`, if using `clone3()`.

Then I considered the bitwise checks in `copy_clone_args_from_user`. With the new code, if `clone3()` passes a negative value like `-1`, `args.exit_signal & ~((u64)CSIGNAL)` returns non-zero, returning `-EINVAL`.

If it passes 255, the check returns zero. Then, in `kernel_clone()`, the check `valid_signal(255)` evaluates to 0, which would return `-EINVAL`. This confirms that for `clone3()`, the behavior for `args.exit_signal` is *exactly* the same as it was previously, at least with regards to `clone3()`.

However, `args.exit_signal` has been previously bitwise-checked to only allow values 0 to 255, so it will *always* fit perfectly into an `int`, and be positive. `kernel_clone()` will call `valid_signal()`. If the value is between 0 and 255, casting it to an `unsigned long` keeps it within that range. The `sig <= _NSIG` check will then work as expected, given that `_NSIG` is 64 on most architectures (sometimes 128). If it's larger, it'll return 0, as intended.

And, as a final check, is it *possible* for `args.exit_signal` to be negative? Nope! The earlier bitwise check `args.exit_signal & ~((u64)CSIGNAL)` enforces that only the lower 8 bits can be set (since `CSIGNAL` is 0x000000ff).     "thought": "**Analyzing the `clone3` System Call Change**

Okay, so I'm looking at this change, specifically at how it handles `args.exit_signal` in the `clone3` system call. The original code in `tools/testing/selftests/clone3/clone3_selftests.h` sets `args.exit_signal = -1`. The question is, what happens if we feed that in?

Initially, `args.exit_signal` is a `u64`, so -1 becomes `0xffffffffffffffff`. The old `copy_clone_args_from_user` function checked this with `args.exit_signal & ~((u64)CSIGNAL)`, and because the bit pattern of -1 has all bits set, it would have rejected it with `-EINVAL`.

Now, the commit moved the `!valid_signal` check. It's no longer in `copy_clone_args_from_user` and is now in `kernel_clone`. But, there's a new check `clone3_args_valid` in `kernel/fork.c`. This new check appears *before* `kernel_clone` and explicitly checks the `exit_signal` if either `CLONE_THREAD` or `CLONE_PARENT` is set:

```c
	if ((kargs->flags & (CLONE_THREAD | CLONE_PARENT)) &&
	    kargs->exit_signal)
		return false;
```
If `exit_signal` was originally invalid (greater than `_NSIG`), it would have been rejected by the old `copy_clone_args_from_user` routine. Now it's initially accepted by `copy_clone_args_from_user` but is rejected by `clone3_args_valid` if `CLONE_THREAD` or `CLONE_PARENT` are set and `exit_signal` is non-zero.  Since `clone3_args_valid` is called before `kernel_clone`, this is fine, it will still result in `-EINVAL`.

Let's trace where `kernel_clone` *is* called. It now happens *after* other checks, including `CLONE_PIDFD`:

```c
	if ((clone_flags & CLONE_PIDFD) &&
	    (clone_flags & CLONE_PARENT_SETTID) &&
	    (args->pidfd == args->parent_tid))
		return -EINVAL;

	if (!valid_signal(args->exit_signal))
		return -EINVAL;
```

Are there any *negative* consequences, though?  Consider `CLONE_INTO_CGROUP`. In `sys_clone3`, the code checks `(args.flags & CLONE_INTO_CGROUP) && (args.cgroup > INT_MAX || usize < CLONE_ARGS_SIZE_VER2)`.  But `kernel_clone` just gets a regular pointer to the `args` struct. Does `valid_signal` have a side effect?  No, it's just a simple comparison (`sig <= _NSIG`).

Could `kernel_clone` leak something that was previously freed or not cleaned up? Let's check the stack frames again. If `kernel_clone` fails, the `kargs` struct is on the stack and only contains pointers to stack variables that don't need to be freed.  `set_tid` is also on the stack.

What about `copy_process`?  `copy_process` could potentially leak if something fails after the arguments are passed. If `copy_process` fails, it returns `ERR_PTR`.  The check on `args->exit_signal` now happens *before* calling `copy_process`, so `copy_process` won't be called with an invalid `exit_signal`.  But what *does* `copy_process` do with `args->exit_signal`? Let's trace it and see…

It seems like the code explicitly validates the signal, which should prevent any problems. But syzkaller flagged a `WARN_ON_ONCE(!valid_signal(sig))` which doesn't make sense if that check is in place.

Alright, the warning is actually in `group_send_sig_info`, which `do_notify_parent` *doesn't* call, so that's a red herring. The problem is in the logic after the call to `do_notify_parent`. Looking closer, there is a check for `if (sig != SIGCHLD)`, which *could* be problematic.

The commit message says invalid exit signals should be rejected with `-EINVAL`. The crash must be because syzkaller simply passed an invalid `exit_signal` to `clone()`, expecting `-EINVAL`, but perhaps it missed this. Let's look at `kernel/fork.c`.     "content": "{"content":"commit 3f2c788a13143620c5471ac96ac4f033fc9ac3f3\nAuthor: Christian Brauner <brauner@kernel.org>\nDate:   Thu May 7 12:32:14 2020 +0200\n\n    fork: prevent accidental access to clone3 features\n    \n    Jan reported an issue where an interaction between sign-extending clone's\n    flag argument on ppc64le and the new CLONE_INTO_CGROUP feature causes\n    clone() to consistently fail with EBADF.\n    \n    The whole story is a little longer. The legacy clone() syscall is odd in a\n    bunch of ways and here two things interact. First, legacy clone's flag\n    argument is word-size dependent, i.e. it's an unsigned long whereas most\n    system calls with flag arguments use int or unsigned int. Second, legacy\n    clone() ignores unknown and deprecated flags. The two of them taken\n    together means that users on 64bit systems can pass garbage for the upper\n    32bit of the clone() syscall since forever and things would just work fine.\n    Just try this on a 64bit kernel prior to v5.7-rc1 where this will succeed\n    and on v5.7-rc1 where this will fail with EBADF:\n    \n    int main(int argc, char *argv[])\n    {\n            pid_t pid;\n    \n            /* Note that legacy clone() has different argument ordering on\n             * different architectures so this won't work everywhere.\n             *\n             * Only set the upper 32 bits.\n             */\n            pid = syscall(__NR_clone, 0xffffffff00000000 | SIGCHLD,\n                          NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL);\n            if (pid < 0)\n                    exit(EXIT_FAILURE);\n            if (pid == 0)\n                    exit(EXIT_SUCCESS);\n            if (wait(NULL) != pid)\n                    exit(EXIT_FAILURE);\n    \n            exit(EXIT_SUCCESS);\n    }\n    \n    Since legacy clone() couldn't be extended this was not a problem so far and\n    nobody really noticed or cared since nothing in the kernel ever bothered to\n    look at the upper 32 bits.\n    \n    But once we introduced clone3() and expanded the flag argument in struct\n    clone_args to 64 bit we opened this can of worms. With the first flag-based\n    extension to clone3() making use of the upper 32 bits of the flag argument\n    we've effectively made it possible for the legacy clone() syscall to reach\n    clone3() only flags. The sign extension scenario is just the odd\n    corner-case that we needed to figure this out.\n    \n    The reason we just realized this now and not already when we introduced\n    CLONE_CLEAR_SIGHAND was that CLONE_INTO_CGROUP assumes that a valid cgroup\n    file descriptor has been given. So the sign extension (or the user\n    accidently passing garbage for the upper 32 bits) caused the\n    CLONE_INTO_CGROUP bit to be raised and the kernel to error out when it\n    didn't find a valid cgroup file descriptor.\n    \n    Let's fix this by always capping the upper 32 bits for all codepaths that\n    are not aware of clone3() features. This ensures that we can't reach\n    clone3() only features by accident via legacy clone as with the sign\n    extension case and also that legacy clone() works exactly like before, i.e.\n    ignoring any unknown flags.  This solution risks no regressions and is also\n    pretty clean.\n    \n    Fixes: 7f192e3cd316 (\"fork: add clone3\")\n    Fixes: ef2c41cf38a7 (\"clone3: allow spawning processes into cgroups\")\n    Reported-by: Jan Stancek <jstancek@redhat.com>\n    Signed-off-by: Christian Brauner <christian.brauner@ubuntu.com>\n    Cc: Arnd Bergmann <arnd@arndb.de>\n    Cc: Dmitry V. Levin <ldv@altlinux.org>\n    Cc: Andreas Schwab <schwab@linux-m68k.org>\n    Cc: Florian Weimer <fw@deneb.enyo.de>\n    Cc: libc-alpha@sourceware.org\n    Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # 5.3+\n    Link: https://sourceware.org/pipermail/libc-alpha/2020-May/113596.html\n    Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20200507103214.77218-1-christian.brauner@ubuntu.com\n"}",

Author: Deepanshu Kartikey <kartikey406@gmail.com>

kernel/fork: validate exit_signal in kernel_clone()

This commit moves the validation of exit_signal from
copy_clone_args_from_user() into kernel_clone() to ensure all callers,
including legacy clone() syscalls, are protected. It now rejects invalid
exit_signal values (values greater than _NSIG) with -EINVAL.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260307064202.353405-1-kartikey406@gmail.com/T/ [v1]
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260316104536.558108-1-kartikey406@gmail.com/T/ [v2]

> diff --git a/kernel/fork.c b/kernel/fork.c
> index 65113a304518..59a790d1b257 100644
> --- a/kernel/fork.c
> +++ b/kernel/fork.c
> @@ -2633,6 +2633,9 @@ pid_t kernel_clone(struct kernel_clone_args *args)
>  	    (args->pidfd == args->parent_tid))
>  		return -EINVAL;
>  
> +	if (!valid_signal(args->exit_signal))
> +		return -EINVAL;
> +

Is the documentation comment directly above kernel_clone() still accurate?

It currently states that args->exit_signal is expected to be checked for
sanity by the caller, but this check shifts the validation responsibility
away from the caller.

Also, does this change introduce a user-visible regression for legacy clone()
callers?

Prior to this patch, the legacy clone() syscall silently accepted invalid
signals up to 255 in the lower byte of clone_flags, treating them as if no
signal should be sent.

By moving the check into kernel_clone(), these previously accepted calls
will now fail with -EINVAL. Could this break existing userspace applications
that inadvertently set bits in the 65-255 range due to uninitialized memory
or buggy bitwise operations?

{"findings":[{"problem":"Rejecting invalid exit_signal values in legacy clone() syscalls violates the kernel's strict no-userspace-regression policy.","severity":"Critical","severity_explanation":"1. Prior to this patch, the legacy `clone()` syscall did not validate that `exit_signal` (the lower 8 bits of `clone_flags`) was a valid signal (<= _NSIG). Invalid values up to 255 were silently accepted and treated as if no signal should be sent (similar to 0).
2. The patch moves the `valid_signal(args->exit_signal)` check into `kernel_clone()`, which applies to all caller paths, including the legacy `clone()` syscall.
3. Returning `-EINVAL` for previously accepted invalid signals constitutes a user-visible behavior change. If any existing userspace application inadvertently sets bits in the range 65-255 in the lower byte of `clone_flags` (due to uninitialized memory, buggy bitwise operations, or relying on undocumented behavior), it will suddenly fail to create processes or threads.
4. This is a direct ABI breakage without proper deprecation, violating the kernel's strict "no regressions in userspace" rule."},{"problem":"The function-level block comment above kernel_clone() incorrectly states that the caller checks args->exit_signal for sanity.","severity":"Low","severity_explanation":"1.
